speaker: interviewee
text: --which I think is terribly important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. It's Tuesday, January the 30th, 1996. I'm Sharon Tyler. I'm interviewing the Reverend Dr. Isaac Levy. We're in London, England. And this is tape 2.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mentioned this, this-- the situation with the survivors in the hospitals. As the days and the weeks passed-- Belsen was no longer regarded as a concentration camp, of course, once it was liberated. It became a Displaced Persons camp. And there were tens of thousands of inmates, not only those who had survived Belsen itself. But Europe, at the time, was-- consisted of masses of people wandering around, going from one camp to another, looking for relatives and looking for, for their nearest and dearest. And so we had people coming into Belsen, going out to Belsen. And this created a severe problem, as far as the military authorities was concerned. The administration was in the hands of military government, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not that the military government of authorities were, in my view, particularly efficient, or did they fully understand the kind of people they were dealing with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they based? Actually, at the-- the military government was based at the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The military government consisted of a detachment of military government in the-- in the camp. They were responsible for administration. It was some major or other, whose name I've forgotten now, who was in charge. But they were responsible-- they were supposed to be responsible for seeing that the food supplies were brought in and all the necessary facilities, as it were. But, I say, they didn't understand the people they were dealing with. And they were not easy people to deal with. That I can understand. After all, they'd been through hell. And they were-- many of them felt that the world owed them a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, for example, they would go out foraging in the locality. They could see Germans were living fairly comfortable. And yet they were in, in, in this situation. Why should they-- they were defeated in war. Why should they enjoy the benefits, as it were?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had to reprimand them and say, foraging is not just foraging. It's stealing. And the time's going to come when you're going to leave this camp, and you're going to go into a civilized society. Get out of the habit of doing this sort of thing. Now, in the army, we used to call it winning. They used to win something, which meant they pinched it. They took it.  They kept it. But this situation could not continue. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The other problem was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a very human problem. And that was-- I'm thinking in terms of the women. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a tremendous, basic, maternal urge on the part of these women, who felt they need to live like normal lives again. Until now, their bodies had not functioned normally. Now they were beginning to function. And with the function, came the, the desire for marriage, for children. They want to say that we can prove that the Jewish people survived. They destroyed so much of us, our families. We will replace the families, as it were. And this was a problem I had to deal with. The women wanted to marry. Men wanted to marry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I had-- for example, a couple came to see me. And they said, we want to get married. I said, have you been married before? They said, yes. Where was your husband? Where was your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came-- we were in Auschwitz. I went to the right, they went to the left, or vice versa. And we knew that if you go over there, that's the end. And there's no survivors, if you've-- once you've-- where Mengele and, and his-- has his 'selections,' as they called them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was faced with this problem. Can I officiate at this wedding? And I got in touch with my religious authorities in London. And I told them the situation. And their answer was categorically no. Until such time as we have a complete list of survivors, you can't because it might-- they might-- their partners might turn up. I thought this was an absurdity beyond words. How long are we going to wait till they get a complete list of survivors? It'll take years. They're traveling all over the place. God knows where they are.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had one case in particular. A man comes to me, shows me a piece of paper on which it says, I so-and-so, the son of so-and-so, take so-and-so's-- the daughter of so-and-so as my wife, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] witnessed by-- and there were two people signed as witnesses. And I said to them, mazel tov, you're married. Common law, if you like, but you're married. And this is what happened. People began to live together as husband and wife. You couldn't stop them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did-- you know, given that you'd been told no, how did you deal, deal with that? You just accepted it, presumably.

speaker: interviewee
text: I looked the other way. What else am I going to do? It's a human issue. People in London didn't appreciate the situation. I mean, you-- it's impossible to visualize it or imagine it until you've seen it on the spot. And, and power of words isn't enough to describe this sort of situation. And these people were beginning to, to feel that they are human again. And it's a wonderful thing to think that they have-- what they've been through, that they're no longer skeletons. They are human beings. And the, the women are beginning to look like women, so much so that I felt inclined to encourage this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I used to flit backwards and forwards from Belsen to Army Headquarters because we were moving on into Germany and to-- ultimately, to the Luneburg Heath when the surrender came. And I used to come back into the officer's mess. And they used to say to me, what's happening over there? They used to call me the 'Beast of Belsen' coming to report. And I remember on one occasion, I said to them, you know what I'd like to do? Perhaps you could help me. I want these women to be given the facilities to look like women. I'd like them to have needle and cotton, knitting needles and wool, lipstick, mirrors, combs, anything that makes a woman feel she's a woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one officer wrote back to his home in Lancashire. And I, in the course of time, received parcels sent to me from this town in Leicestershire, in which-- in Lancashire-- each item had a little label attached to it. This comes to you-- you don't know us, but from a well-wisher. And I was deeply moved by this. It was a wonderful gesture. And I corresponded with the, the fellow who, who-- at the other end-- who had written to the local newspaper. And as a result of his letter to the paper, people were responding. And I felt, well, that's-- the human feelings are still there. I don't give up hope for, for humanity. And this was the sort of feeling. And so the women began to feel that they were women again. But to give an idea-- example of an idea, of how the military people didn't appreciate this situation, I was called in by the major in charge of the camp, who said to me, you know, these people are behaving very badly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He found a woman had cut up an army blanket and made a dress of it-- out of it. But what she didn't know-- that the letters 'WD' stand for 'War Department.' And this was right in the back of the dress, the letters WD. And this major said, they are abusing army property, misusing army property. I said to him, come off it. Come off it. You know, she's actually made herself a dress. She-- she's been living in these horrible pajamas that the Nazis had dressed them in. Now she wants to behave like a human being. Well, they mustn't use Army property. Well, we laughed it off. But this was the sort of attitude. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] But something else was happening in the camp, which I find-- I still, as I think of it, is quite remarkable. The power of survival is quite amazing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They-- in Belsen, they established a Central Jewish Committee. One dynamic little man by the name of Yossele Rosensaft was the moving, moving figure in this. He was indestructible. He'd been pushed from one camp to another and survived. He stood about 5 foot 2 in his socks, but energy beyond recognition. And he organized this committee. And they had several departments-- one on hygiene, one on culture, one on the secretariat recording, so that they should record the names of survivors and so on, a highly organized government within a government, as it were, inside the camp. And they presented the authorities with a fait accompli. If you want to know anything about the organization inside this camp, you come to us. So the military had to ask them how to organize themselves inside the camp. Because each hut inside these barracks that they were living had its own sort of representative.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a perfect democracy inside the camp. They even organized their own cultural activities. They discovered they had a number of actors. And so they had a theater. They had a newspaper for a little while called All this within the framework of a matter of weeks after the liberation-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so it was quite an organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But there was a big 'but' as well. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The military authorities were anxious to empty the camp. And the only way to do it was to send people back to their native countries. Hungarians, go back to Hungary. The few French who were there, glad to go back to France. One element refused to budge-- the Jews who came from Poland. And they were the largest number. They were the majority.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said, oh, no. There's no going back to Poland. 'Do we go back to the cemetery of our people?' was their argument. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a-- an immovable force meeting an impossible object.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did they do? The military authorities decided to send a group of them to another camp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went to see this camp, a place called Lingen-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ghastly, worse than Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: The facilities were impossible. The accommodation was unbelievably bad. And we protested that this is not the way to treat these people. So they sent them back to Belsen. And I can understand they wanted to empty the camp because they wanted to make room for-- perhaps, for the invalids or whatever it was. But there was this question of repatriation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Our people, our Jews from Poland, said no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where do you want to go? There's only one country-- Palestine. Whether they'd ever been Zionists is not the point. But there was no other country would have them. England didn't take anybody. America wouldn't have any more. The gates were barred and bolted. So where do they go? To sit there and rot? So there was no way out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to come back to England for a brief visit [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to report to the local Jewish community what was happening. I held a meeting at the headquarters of the Rothschild house. And I painted a picture of what I'd seen in Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I looked around at these representatives of Anglo Jewry, and I thought to myself, they are looking as though I'm telling them something they've never heard about. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They hadn't heard of what was going on inside Germany? I didn't get much reaction. So I went to see Sir Herbert Emerson, who was the High Commissioner for Refugees. He received me very nicely. I explained the situation. And I said, I'm particularly concerned about the future of these people. Where are they going to go from here? There's only one place. They want to go to Palestine-- particularly younger element want to go  to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He looked very sad, raised his hands in horror and said, I'm terribly sorry. I sympathize with you completely. But you know the entry to Palestine is only by certificates issued by the mandatory authorities. And there aren't any available. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said to him, you know, if I were to go back to Belsen, say to these young people, 'You're stuck; Palestine is closed for you,' I don't guarantee they won't hang themselves from the rafters of the huts. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He said, I'm terribly sorry. So I left empty-handed. I was in London. So I thought I must go and see one person in particular. I went to see Dr. Weizmann, who was the, spokesman of Anglo Jewry as far as the world was concerned, highly respected, we thought, by the government, the leader of the Zionist movement. I went to see him. He was an old, sick man at the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went to him. And I told him the situation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He listened. He's going grayer and grayer. He said to me, my boy, there was a time when I entered the colonial office through the front door. Now I go through the back door like a schnorrer, like a beggar. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, I don't know whether I controlled myself enough, but I felt as though I was drowning myself in tears. The great fightsman, the man who the world looked to as the leader of the Zionist movement, he, too, was helpless. His hands were tied. Well, I went back to Belsen. I didn't dare tell them. But one thing I did know, that they were determined to get there whatever happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember one young fellow-- he couldn't have been more, I don't know, perhaps he was 16, 17, maybe-- came over to me and he said, I just want to say good-bye. I said, where are you going? I'm going back to Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, what for? I want to see whether there's anything left of my family. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, I wish you luck. A few weeks later, he was back, bright and chirpy. I said, well? There's nothing there. But I found one thing. I found the way across Europe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was the way they went across Europe-- I called 'the Underground'-- to go find their way across to Italy, or whatever the case may be, where they would ultimately go on this what are called so-called illegal immigration. And that's how some of these young people disappeared. And there were some very unusual people amongst these young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on. Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I'll give an example. There was a group of young girls, whom I actually met inside the death camp, who had very recently arrived. They were from Carpatho-Russia, Hungarian-- who spoke beautiful Hebrew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had been the graduates of a Hebrew-speaking gymnasium in a place called Munkacs, which is in Carpatho-Russia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They managed to clean up their corner in which they were living. They had recently arrived. So they were a pretty healthy state. And I remember on a Saturday I was-- we got together. We sang Hebrew songs. And we talked. And I told them about my experiences in the Middle East. I'd been to Palestine. I knew something about the country. And I knew that these were the kind of people that wouldn't stay in the camp. A, they had the equipment. They spoke the language. And they had all the enthusiasm. And I'm sure that ultimately, I, I, I couldn't follow the movements of every individual in the camp. But I'm sure that they wouldn't have stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was the sort of atmosphere that existed. This whole problem of, of rehabilitation was a tremendous problem. Repatriation was really-- this was the bugbear. How do we get rid of the people? How do we send them back? And I give you another problem which I had had. There were one or two Jews originally from Germany, who managed to survive-- and still in the camp. And I wrote to the authorities and said, what is the future of these people? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If they go back to their native towns, how will they be treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the answer I got shook me, too. They said, as long as they are in the camp, they'll be treated like, like survivors. Once they go back to their native cities in Germany, they will be treated like Germans, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which was irrespective of what they'd been through, the fact that they had been victims of Nazi Germany. And this was brought home to me very, very, very vividly. I had the opportunity to go to Berlin. It was part of my area because-- the British Zone of occupied Berlin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I arrived there. And I looked for Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The synagogues had been destroyed, of course, in the Kristallnacht of November '38-- '37. For what? I found, on a Saturday morning, Jews were having a service in the hall attached to one of these synagogues that had been destroyed.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was deeply moved, the fact that they had been survivors. They-- many of them had been in Theresienstadt and had come back to Germany, in Berlin. And I said to them, look, I'm only here for a day or so. If you want to communicate with anybody anywhere in the world to tell them you're alive, I will collect your letters tomorrow morning. And I will distribute them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I collected them on the Sunday morning. And then I got back to my office. I separated them according to countries-- to Palestine-- it was easy-- South Africa, to England. There was a sort of a-- there was a unit that was responsible for tracing people. And I sent them off in batches.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I heard no more, until one bright day I was hauled up before my local chief chaplain, who said to me, you have been breaking the law. Now what have I done? Apparently, somebody had received such a letter, wrote to a relative in Switzerland to say that they had heard so-and-so was still alive in Berlin. And if you want to communicate with them, send me the letter, and I will send it off. So I was accused by military-- this was intercepted by military intelligence. And they duly-- I was duly reprimanded. I was breaking the laws of censorship and whatnot, acting as a sort of unofficial postman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I said to my chief, who was my superior in rank, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] do you mind if I speak to you as a clergyman to clergyman? Or must I speak to you as a major to a colonel? Well, he said, of course, I'm a clergyman first. And I said, do you know what these people wrote? I am alive. I have survived. No military intelligence involved-- a purely humanitarian gesture. And all he could say to me was, the trouble with you, Levy, is you're too much of a Jewish nationalist.

speaker: interviewee
text: What's nationalism has got to do with it, I don't know. But this was the sort of attitude. Again, an example of how they didn't understand the situation. I had another example, where I-- the-- Montgomery, as the commander in chief, had issued a decree-- no fraternization with the German population. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then, shortly afterwards, the fraternization order was withdrawn. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I wrote to my chief in London, and I sent a copy to my local chief in Germany, saying that this was a move which I strongly disapprove. Two things will happen. If the British soldier meets-- fraternizes with the German girls, two things are likely to happen. One, he'll probably be indoctrinated that the Germans aren't so bad after all. And secondly, there will be an increase of venereal disease the like of which we have not seen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I got severely reprimanded. What right have you got to criticize an order coming from the commander in chief? You will withdraw that letter. Write to London, saying-- because I wrote to London saying, pass this on to the War Office.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my chief in London, not a very strong character, didn't do anything about it. I had to write to him and-- saying, I withdraw that letter. So I wrote the letter-- tore up the original and gave the copy to my local chief. Nothing happened. My conscience was clear, but nothing happened. Again, it was an example of the sort of frustrations we had to put up with because I was the sort of go-between between the, the Jews, survivors, and the authorities. They had no one else to talk to. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Another example of the kind of situation we had to put up with. We're talking about the Jews of Berlin. Whilst I was there, I had the opportunity of investigating the situation. What happens to these people when they come back from concentration camps, go back into civilian life?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Peculiar situation-- in, in Berlin at the time, they had a ruling that they divided-- they supplied people according to a ration scale. The highest ration scale were called [NON-ENGLISH], the 'victims of fascism.' And they were given a higher rate of scale.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it depended on whether you were fit to labor, to work. The Jews who came back were what? Not in a fit state to do heavy manual labor. And they were put on the lowest possible ration scale. So I wrote to the British officer and commander in Berlin and said, this is unfair. If anybody is a victim of fascism, it's these people. They should be put on a higher scale.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he, he accepted my message. But unfortunately, I don't know whether, whether anything transpired because I was only passing through, as it were. But I did write to London, pointing out to the Board of Deputies, for example, and pointed out this was the situation, and perhaps it ought to be known. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, these were the sort of things we were up against. There was one memorable occasion which I must remember. We had a visit in Belsen [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from Earl Harrison, an American, who came to Belsen [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on a special commission from the president of the United States. He was going on a tour of Europe. And he was interviewed. And he was meeting people. He came to Belsen. And Yossele Rosensaft, in his own usual way, acted as spokesman.
